question: In a distributed caching system with multiple nodes, how does consistent hashing distribute keys differently than simple modulo (hash(key) % N) hashing, and what happens to cached data when a server is removed from the cluster?
answer: Consistent hashing maps keys and node identifiers onto a circular hash ring using the same hash function. A key is assigned to the first node encountered clockwise from its hash position. When a node is removed, only the keys between that node and its predecessor must be reassigned — roughly 1/N of total keys. Modulo hashing (hash(key) % N) remaps nearly all keys when N changes, triggering cascading cache stampede. Virtual nodes improve balance. Used by Cassandra, DynamoDB, and Discord.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  Key["Key: user:42"] --> Hash["hash('user:42') = position on ring"]
  Hash --> Assign["Walk clockwise to first node"]
  Assign --> Found["Server-B (normal operation)"]

  Remove["Node Server-C is removed"] --> Local["Only keys in arc between Server-C and its predecessor remap"]
  Local --> Result["~1/N keys reassigned; remaining keys untouched"]

  Modulo["hash(key) % N"] --> ModResult["N changes from 3 to 2 — nearly all keys remap"]
  ModResult --> Stampede["Cache stampede: all clients miss simultaneously, database overload"]

  style Found fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
  style Result fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
  style Stampede fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
